title: Multilingual Dad Joke Worked Example
goal: Use a multi-model experiment panel and multi-model judge panel to create a dad joke that works in English, French, Spanish, and Japanese.

model_experiment_panels.harness: GitHub Copilot task agents with model overrides
model_experiment_panels.purpose: Generate competing candidates from model-diverse language strategies before synthesis.

judges.role: cross-language equivalence and concise joke structure
groan factor, brevity, and tradeoff analysis

tracks.label: Run A - GPT-style generator
tracks.hypothesis: Globally standardized double meanings can be robust across languages.

tracks.label: Run B - Gemini-style generator
tracks.hypothesis: Universal physical setups can avoid language-specific puns.

tracks.label: Run C - Claude-style generator
tracks.hypothesis: Conceptual ambiguity can produce stronger dad-joke misdirection.

tracks.label: Run D - cross-model synthesis
tracks.hypothesis: Judge dissent can guide a polished final candidate.

iterations.hypothesis: A globally adopted technical double meaning can survive all four languages with minimal localization loss.

commands.build: Generate or synthesize a multilingual joke candidate from the model experiment panel.
commands.judge: Check text completeness, then aggregate independent multi-model judge notes.

output.title: Computer virus
output.concept: Object personification plus literal computer-virus/medical-virus double meaning.

joke.en: Why did the computer go to the doctor? It had a virus.
joke.fr: Pourquoi l'ordinateur est-il allé chez le médecin ? Il avait un virus.
joke.es: ¿Por qué fue la computadora al médico? Tenía un virus.
joke.ja: なぜコンピューターはお医者さんに行ったの？ウイルスにかかったから。

scores.notes: All four language variants are present.
Strongest aggregate equivalence, but some risk that the joke is familiar and less fresh.

lesson.trigger: A globally adopted technical double meaning can survive all four languages with minimal localization loss.
lesson.action: Keep globally standardized double meanings, but polish native-script translations and reduce fixture-style romanization.
lesson.evidence: gpt-generator/loop-01-gpt-virus/joke.md, candidate.json, and model judge notes

iterations.hypothesis: A universal spatial setup can avoid language-specific puns and still feel dad-joke simple.

commands.build: Generate or synthesize a multilingual joke candidate from the model experiment panel.
commands.judge: Check text completeness, then aggregate independent multi-model judge notes.

output.title: Meet at the corner
output.concept: Anthropomorphized walls meet at a corner, which is both architectural geometry and a meeting place.

joke.en: What did one wall say to the other wall? I'll meet you at the corner.
joke.fr: Que dit un mur à un autre mur ? « On se retrouve au coin. »
joke.es: ¿Qué le dice una pared a otra pared? « Nos vemos en la esquina. »
joke.ja: ひとつの壁がもうひとつの壁に何と言った？「角で待ち合わせよう。」

scores.notes: All four language variants are present.
One judge preferred this candidate for charm and universality, but two judges saw slightly weaker translation equivalence.

lesson.trigger: A universal spatial setup can avoid language-specific puns and still feel dad-joke simple.
lesson.action: Preserve the wall/corner idea as a charming runner-up; watch for corner-word nuance across languages.

iterations.hypothesis: A conceptual ambiguity between body locations and geographic locations can work without phonetic wordplay.

commands.build: Generate or synthesize a multilingual joke candidate from the model experiment panel.
commands.judge: Check text completeness, then aggregate independent multi-model judge notes.

output.title: Two places
output.concept: The doctor interprets 'two places' as travel destinations instead of fracture locations.

joke.en: I told my doctor I broke my arm in two places. He told me to stop going to those places.
joke.fr: J'ai dit à mon médecin que je m'étais cassé le bras en deux endroits. Il m'a dit d'arrêter d'aller dans ces endroits.
joke.es: Le dije al médico que me había roto el brazo en dos lugares. Me dijo que dejara de ir a esos lugares.
joke.ja: 「先生、2か所で腕を骨折しました」と言ったら、「じゃあ、その場所には行かないようにしなさい」と言われました。

scores.notes: All four language variants are present.
Best groan factor, but weakest brevity and naturalness across the four languages.

lesson.trigger: A conceptual ambiguity between body locations and geographic locations can work without phonetic wordplay.
lesson.action: Keep conceptual ambiguity in mind, but avoid long setups that become forced in Japanese and Romance-language translations.

iterations.hypothesis: Use the panel's aggregate winner, keep the globally shared virus double meaning, and polish the wording into native scripts.

commands.build: Generate or synthesize a multilingual joke candidate from the model experiment panel.
commands.judge: Check text completeness, then aggregate independent multi-model judge notes.

output.title: Polished universal-virus joke
output.concept: A computer gets medical advice because it has a virus; the biological/digital double meaning is shared across all target languages.

joke.en: Why did the computer go to the doctor? It had a virus.
joke.fr: Pourquoi l'ordinateur est-il allé chez le médecin ? Il avait un virus.
joke.es: ¿Por qué fue la computadora al médico? Tenía un virus.
joke.ja: なぜコンピューターはお医者さんに行ったの？ウイルスにかかったから。

scores.notes: All four language variants are present.
Panel consensus after polishing: the joke is not the freshest, but it is the most equal across all four languages.

lesson.trigger: Use the panel's aggregate winner, keep the globally shared virus double meaning, and polish the wording into native scripts.
lesson.action: Stop: the candidate is brief, globally portable, and equally legible after native-script polishing.

iterations.stop_reason: Synthesis reached the best cross-language equality score.

best.why: Best weighted panel score while passing the four-language completeness gate.

rules.trigger: Multilingual language tasks can reward stale but highly portable jokes.
rules.action: Record judge dissent explicitly so readers can see the tradeoff between freshness and cross-language equality.

synthesis: The generator panel produced three different strategies: a globally shared virus double meaning, a universal wall/corner spatial joke, and a stronger-groan doctor ambiguity. The judge panel disagreed, with one model preferring the wall/corner candidate for charm and two models preferring the virus candidate for equal translation. The synthesis loop accepted the aggregate winner and polished the Japanese into native script while preserving the exact joke mechanic.
